texts.orig: Permeability  coefficients P for He, 0,, N,, CO,  CH,,  C,H,, C,H,, and C3H,  in  12 different silicone polymer membranes were determined at 35.0'C and pressures up to 9 atm. Values of F for CO,,  CH,,  and C,H, were also determined at 10.0 and 55.0'C. In  addition, mean  diffusion coefficients D and solubility coefficients S were obtained for CO,,  CH,,  and C3H,  in 6 silicone polymers at 10.0, 35 . 0 , and 55.0'C. Substitution of  increasingly bulkier functional groups in  the side and  backbone chains o f   silicone  polymers results in  a significant decrease in P for a  given penetrant gas. This is due mainly to a decrease in E, whereas S decreases to a much lesser extent. Backbone substitutions appear to have a somewhat lesser effect in depressing than equivalent side-chain substitutions.  The  selectivity of  a silicone  membrane for a gas A relative to a gas €3, i.e., the permeability ratio &A)/&€%), may increase or decrease as a result of  such substitutions, but only if  the substituted groups are sufficiently bulky. The selectivity of  the more highly  permeable silicone  membranes is  controlled  by  the  ratio S(A)/S(H). whereas  the  selectivity  of the  less permeable membranes depends on both the ratios D(A)/D(B) and S(A)/S(R). The permeability as well as the selectivity o f   one silicone membrane toward CO, were significantly enhanced by the substitution  of a  fluorine-containing side  group  that  increased  the  solubility  of  CO, in  that polymer.

texts.orig: Remarkable progress has been made during the last decade in the technology of  gas separation by selective permeation through polymer membranes.'-' Whereas up to the middle 1960s this technology was only being studied in the laboratory  or on  pilot-plant  scale, scores of  permeation  plants  are presently separating various gas mixtures on an industrial scale both in this country and abroad. Some of  these plants are o f   substantial size and process gas streams as large as 1 x lo6 to 5 x lo7  ft3/day.

texts.orig: The membranes used at present for the separation of   gas mixtures are made from glassy polymers, such as polysulfone, polyimides, cellulose  triacetate, and ethyl cellulose. These polymers, which  originally were synthesized  for other applications, exhibit a high selectivity for certain gases of   industrial  interest, but their intrinsic gas permeability is very low. However, very high gas fluxes can be obtained through membranes made from such polymers by the obvious expedient  of greatly  reducing  the  membrane  thickness. texts.orig: The above techniques of producing membranes for gas separation processes are  reaching  the  limit of their  development  potential.  Further  significant progress in membrane separation technology will be possible only through the synthesis of new polymer membranes that exhibit both a high selectivity for specific  gases  and  a  high  intrinsic  permeability.  Such  progress  is  greatly impeded  at  the  present  time  by  a  lack  o f sufficient understanding  of the relationships  between the chemical structure of  polymers and their  gas permeability. As a result, new polymer membranes for desired separation processes are currently being developed mainly with the aid of  some empirical principles and by trial-and-error.

texts.orig: Very few significant studies on the structure-permeability  relationships of polymers have been published in the literature (see, for example, Refs. 11-16). The objective of  the present study was, therefore, to generate further information  on these relationships. Accordingly, permeability  coefficients for He, 0,, N,, CO,, CH,,  C,H,, C,H,, and  C,H, in 12 different  silicone  polymer membranes were measured at 350°C and at pressures up to 9 atm. Measurements with  CO,,  CH,,  and  C,H, were  also  made  at  10.0  and  550°C.  In addition, diffusion coefficients for CO,,  CH,,  and C,H, in six o f   the polymer membranes were determined from the permeability coefficients in conjunction with gas solubility data reported  elsewhere.'  Silicone polymers were selected for this study because of  their relatively high permeability, and also because a large variety of  functional groups can be substituted in the side and backbone chains of  these polymers.

texts.orig: Permeability  coefficients were obtained  from steady-state rates of  gas permeation through the silicone polymer membranes. The permeation rates were measured  by  means  of a  permeability  apparatus  of the  variable-volume A diagram of  this apparatus is shown in  Figure  1. The apparatus consists of   two horizontal stainless-steel flanges A which are 6 in. in diameter and 9/16  in. thick. Circular depressions are machined in each flange, so that a cylindrical  cavity  is  formed  when  the  flanges  are  superimposed  with  the depressions  facing  one  another.  The  membrane  to  be  studied  is  clamped between the two flanges by means of  six equally spaced bolts. The membrane is  supported  by  several sheets o f filter  paper  on  top  o f   a  metal  screen; the filter paper and screen are cut to fit the circular depression in the lower flange. A synthetic  rubber  gasket  ensures a  pressure-tight  seal  between  membrane and flanges. Filter  paper  also fills the space between  the membrane and the upper flange. The effective diameter of  the membrane is 3 in., which is that o f the cylindrical cavity formed by the superimposed flanges.

texts.orig: in  the center  of  the upper  flange  into  a  vertical  precision-bore capillary  E made of  borosilicate glass, which is connected to that aperture by means of  a fitting provided with a rubber O-ring. The capillary is open to the atmosphere but  contains  a  short  mercury  plug F that  is  displaced  upward by the permeating  gas. An S-shaped' bend in  the base of  the capillary  prevents the mercury plug from falling into the apparatus. The rate of  displacement of  this plug  offers  a  direct  measure  o f the  rate of gas  permeation  through  the membrane. A smooth  movement  of the  mercury  plug  in  the  capillary  is achieved  by  attaching an electromechanical vibrator  G  to  the  top  of the capillary  and by  coating the inner wall of the capillary  with a  thin  layer  o f silicone oil. The apparatus is also provided with vent lines H and I, which are made of  stainless steel tubing and are provided with needle valves of  the same material.

texts.orig: The purging operation is terminated  by closing vent line I. The permeating gas  thus  flows  into  capillary E against  atmospheric  pressure  and  causes mercury  plug  F to rise in that capillary. The position of  the mercury  plug is then  monitored  with  a  Gaertner  cathetometer.  Steady-state  permeation  is achieved when the plug displacement is a linear function o f   time. It should be noted that the pressure p h on the high-pressure (lower) side of  the apparatus can be varied at will, whereas the pressure pI on the low-pressure (upper) side of  the apparatus is always near atmospheric pressure. Repeat measurements are performed  rapidly by  opening vent line I, thus causing the mercury  plug to drop back  to the S-shaped base of  the capillary, and then closing the vent line and again monitoring the rate o f   rise of  mercury plug F in the capillary. A wide range of  permeation rates can be measured with the above apparatus by an appropriate choice of  capillary diameter and penetrant  gas pressure.

texts.orig: The  permeation  of gases  through  polymer  membranes  at  temperatures above their  glass transition, as was the case in  the present  study, generally occurs by a 'solution-diffusion' mechanism. This mechanism postulates that the permeation process is controlled by the molecular diffusion of  the penetrant gas in the membrane matrix, while, at the same time, the penetrant dissolved at the membrane interfaces is in  solution equilibrium  with  its adjoining gas phase.20
texts.text: The  permeation  of gases  through  polymer  membranes  at  temperatures above their  glass transition, as was the case in  the present  study, generally occurs by a 'solution-diffusion' mechanism. This mechanism postulates that the permeation process is controlled by the molecular diffusion of  the penetrant gas in the membrane matrix, while, at the same time, the penetrant dissolved at the membrane interfaces is in  solution equilibrium  with  its adjoining gas phase.20

These results probably  reflect  the relatively  high diffusivity o f   He in  polymers (compared with that o f   N,,  0,, or CH,),  which is due to the small size of  the He atom. The substitution of  bulkier  functional  groups in  the polymer side chains or backbones decreases the diffusivity of  all penetrants, but the diffusivity o f   He decreases relatively less than that o f   the other penetrants.  It should be noted that polymers A-F  have increasingly bulkier  side chains, whereas  polymers G-L  have increasingly bulkier groups in the repeating units of  their backbone chains.

texts.orig: 2.  Polymer  E  and, to  a  lesser  extent, polymers  F, G, K, and  L  exhibit a higher  permeability  to  CO,, relative  to  other  penetrant  gases,  than  anticipated. In the case of   polymer E, this is clearly the result o f   a solubility effect. As is shown el~ewhere,'~ the solubility of CO, in polymer E, [(CF3CH,CH,)CH3SiO],, is anomalously high. It is speculated that this high solubility might be caused by specific interactions between CO,  and the polar, fluorine-containing groups in the side chains o f   polymer E. Hence, the permeability of  a polymer to a given penetrant can be  enhanced, relative to other penetrants, by the substitution of  specific atoms or functional groups in the polymer.

texts.orig: I t   is  Seen  that  the values  o f the mean  permeability  coefficient decrease markedly as increasingly  bulkier  functional  groups  are  substituted  in  the polymer side chains or backbone chain. This decrease in is due mainly to a decrease in penetrant diffusivity, which is caused, in  turn, by  the increasing rigidity of  the polymer backbone and the decreasing free volume available for the diffusion of  penetrant molecules. It should be noted that the substitution of bulkier  functional  groups  raises  the  glass-transition  temperature  of the polymers, cf. Table I. The solubility of  the penetrant gases is also reduced by such  substitutions,  but  this  effect  is  not as great17 as the  corresponding decrease in penetrant diffusivity.

texts.orig: The effect o f polymer structure on the diffusion coefficients is very significant. Figures 23 and 24 show that the substitution of  bulky functional groups in  the side chains o f silicone polymers causes a  substantial  decrease in  the values of  the diffusion coefficients for C,H8. As another example, at 35.0"C the value of  the diffusion coefficient for C,H8  in polymer F, (C,H,CH,SiO),, is only about 1/10 o f that for C3H8  in polymer A, [(CH,),SiO],. This effect  i s observed  with  all  penetrant  gases  but  is  more  pronounced  the  larger  the molecular  size of the penetrant  and  the bulkier  the functional  groups substituted in the polymers. Similar effects are found for backbone substitutions.

texts.orig: Polymers H, I, and J contain progressively longer alkyl groups substituted between  the siloxane (Si-0) linkages and  exhibit  correspondingly  lower  gas permeabilities. This decrease is, however, relatively small compared  with  the decrease in permeability  from polymer J to polymer G. The relatively low  gas permeability of  polymer G, [(CH3),SiCH,],, is very likely due to the absence of  flexible siloxane linkages. It is interesting to note that the presence of  only one siloxane  linkage  for  every  eight  repeating  ethylene  units  in  polymer  J greatly  enhances the chain flexibility. This is evidenced by  the fact that the gas permeability of  polymer J is about twice that o f   polymer G, which has no siloxane linkages.

texts.orig: Table I1 also shows that a marked difference exists in the gas permeabilities of  polymers K and L, the values of p for various gases in the former polymer being significantly higher than in the latter. This difference is due to the fact that polymer K is amorphous, whereas polymer L, its isomer, is semicrystalline.  The presence o f   crystalline domains in  a  polymer  matrix  decreases the effective permeation area and increases the tortuosity of  the diffusion paths of the penetrant  molecules. Additionally, the mobility  of  polymer chains entering  crystalline  domains  is  reduced  near  the  domain  interfaces.  All  these factors cause  a  decrease in  the diffusion coefficients, and  hence  also  in  the permeability  coefficients, for different  penetrants  in polymer  L, as compared with  its metu isomer, polymer  K.  The solubilities  of  specific penetrants  in polymers K and L probably are more similar, if  only the amorphous domains of  the polymers are considered.

texts.orig: Table V also shows that the value of  a*(CH,/He)  is actually reversed when an  appropriately  bulky  group  is  substituted  in  the  side  chains  o f silicone polymers. For example, CH,  permeates more than twice as fast as He through polymers A-D  [a*(CH,/He) = 2.2-2.51, whereas He  permeates  faster  than CH, through polymer E [a*(CH,/He) = 0.761.  This behavior arises from the fact that He exhibits a  low  solubility  and  a  high  diffusivity in  all  polymers owing to, respectively,  its very  low  critical  temperature  and  small  atomic diameter. texts.orig: The following conclusions can be drawn from the results of  this study:
texts.text: The following conclusions can be drawn from the results of  this study:

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 1. The permeability of   a given silicone polymer to different penetrant gases generally  decreases with  decreasing penetrant  solubility,  particularly  in  the ca s e of   the more highly permeable polymers. Exceptions to this behavior are found for small penetrant molecules and atoms, such as He, and when specific interactions occur between penetrant and polymer.
texts.text: The permeability of   a given silicone polymer to different penetrant gases generally  decreases with  decreasing penetrant  solubility,  particularly  in  the ca s e of   the more highly permeable polymers. Exceptions to this behavior are found for small penetrant molecules and atoms, such as He, and when specific interactions occur between penetrant and polymer.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 2.  The substitution of  bulkier functional  groups in the side and backbone chains of  silicone polymers  decreases substantially  the  gas  permeability  o f these  polymers  by  decreasing  the  diffusivity  o f the  penetrant  gases.  The permeability  to  larger  penetrant  molecules is  decreased  more  than  that  to smaller  ones.  A  similar  effect  results  from  the  substitution  of  Si-0 bonds with stiffer C-H bonds in the backbone chains.

A  similar  effect  results  from  the  substitution  of  Si-0 bonds with stiffer C-H bonds in the backbone chains.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 5.  The substitution of functional groups in the backbone chains of   silicone polymers has the same general effect on polymer permeability and selectivity as the  substitution  of  such  groups  in  the  polymer  side  chains.  However, substitution of  a bulky group in the polymer backbone appears to decrease the permeability  of  the polymer to a  lesser extent  than  the substitution of  the same group in the polymer side chains. Exceptions to the above behavior may occur where specific interactions exist between  a penetrant  and the polymer.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 6.  The permeability  of   a silicone polymer to a  given  penetrant, as well as the  selectivity  o f the  polymer  toward  that  penetrant,  can  be  significantly enhanced  by  the substitution in  the polymer of  a  suitable functional  group that will  induce specific interactions  with  the penetrant.  Such  interactions increase primarily the solubility of   the penetrant in the polymer.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The selectivity of  a silicone polymer can also be enhanced by  the substitution of  bulky functional groups in the side or backbone chains of  the polymer. This affects primarily  the difcsioity of   penetrants. The increase in selectivity resulting  from  such  substitutions  is  most  often  due  to  the  fact  that  the diffusivity of  small penetrant molecules is lowered to a lesser extent than that of large  ones.  In  other  words,  the  polymer  matrix  then  acts  more  like  a 'molecular sieve.' This type of   selectivity  enhancement,  i.e.,  by  changes in relative  diffusivity, probably  will  be  limited  to mixtures  o f   penetrants  that vary greatly in molecular size and shape.

In  other  words,  the  polymer  matrix  then  acts  more  like  a 'molecular sieve.' This type of   selectivity  enhancement,  i.e.,  by  changes in relative  diffusivity, probably  will  be  limited  to mixtures  o f   penetrants  that vary greatly in molecular size and shape.
